rulings.Case Title: FRANK LAGODZINSKI SR VS. GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Substituting Alex Lagodzinski As Successor-In-Interest To, And Substituting Successor-In-Interest For, Deceased Plaintiff; For Leave To File First Amended Complaint; And To Add Previously Sued Roe Defendant To The First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 28, 2025 in Department 304, Line 1. Petitioner's Motion for Order Substituting Alex Lagodzinski, as Successor-in-Interest to, and Substituting Successor-in-Interest for, Deceased Plaintiff; for Leave to File First Amended Complaint; to Add Legal Heirs (1) Alex Lagodzinski, (2) Frank Lagodzinski Jr, and (3) Jayme Ladogzinski as wrongful death plaintiffs, to Add Deborah Roe 1 as a defendant; to Add Existing Defendants as Parties to the First through Fourth Causes of Action; (1) General Electric Company; (2) Air & Liquid Systems Corporation; (3) Asbestos Corporation Limited; (4) Redco Corporation (FKA Crane Co.); (5) Imo Industries LLC; (6) Metropolitan Life Insurance Company; and (7) Paramount Global, F/K/A Viacomcbs Inc., F/K/A Cbs Corporation, A Delaware Corporation, F/K/A Viacom Inc., Successor By Merger To Cbs Corporation, A Pennsylvania Corporation, F/K/A Westinghouse Electric Corporation; and to Add Existing Defendants as Parities to the Fist through Sixth Causes of Action; (1) Foster Wheeler LLC (FKA Foster Wheeler Corporation); and (2) Thomas Dee Engineering Co., Inc. is GRANTED under C.C.P. Sections 377.20, 377.60, 377.62(b), and 473. No opposition filed. As of the date of the granting of Petitioner's motion, the First Amended Complaint is deemed served on all Defendants who have previously appeared. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on January 28, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LINDA L. FEE ET AL VS. BLOCK DRUG COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 28, 2025 in Department 304, Line 2. (Part 1 of 5) 1. Defendant LVMH Perfumes and Cosmetic's Inc., Demurrer to Plaintiffs' Complaint is OVVERULED, in part, and SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND, in part. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. Defendant's request for judicial notice, Exhibit 1, is granted. (C.C.P. Sections 451, 452, 453.) As Plaintiffs point out in their Opposition, Defendant's motion is duplicative of issues already addressed and resolved by this Court in its prior ruling in the matter of Christina Ingels as Successor-in-Interest to Lita Fee vs. BWDAC et al., Case No. CGC-24-277251, on January 3, 2025. The Court finds that the issues raised in this Motion are identical to those previously adjudicated. The Court emphasizes that relitigating issues already ruled upon wastes judicial resources and unnecessarily delays the resolution of this case. Defendant is cautioned against bringing further motions raising issues already decided without a proper showing under the applicable standards. A demurrer tests the legal sufficiency of the complaint. (C.C.P. Section 430.10(e); Rakestraw v. California Physicians' Serv. (2000) 81 Cal.App.4th 39, 42-43.) To withstand a demurrer, a plaintiff must show that the complaint alleges facts sufficient to establish every element of each cause of action. (Id. at p. 43.) In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.) First Cause of Action The demurrer to the First Cause of Action for Negligence on the ground of failure to state a cause of action is overruled. Defendant argues that Plaintiffs' Complaint does not identify any facts to support Plaintiffs' claim that Defendant was negligent. The Court disagrees. In order to establish liability on a negligence theory, a plaintiff must prove duty, breach, causation, and damages. (Conroy v. Regents of University of California (2009) 45 Cal.4th 1244, 1250; Ortega v. Kmart Corp. (2001) 26 Cal. 4th 1200, 1205.) Plaintiffs allege that Decedent was exposed to asbestos from talcum power products and/or talc that was used in the manufacture of those products. (See Exhibit A to Plaintiffs' Complaint.) Plaintiffs allege that Defendant had a duty to exercise due care in pursuance of its activities (the supply of asbestos-containing products) and breached that duty of care by negligently and carelessly placing those products into the stream of commerce and failed to warn of known dangers. (Complaint, 20-36.) Plaintiffs further allege that as a proximate result of this negligence, Plaintiffs suffered permanent injuries, including, mesothelioma, mental, physical, and emotional distress, loss of the ability to perform household services, loss of income, a diminishment of earning potential, and other pecuniary losses. (Id. at 15-18.) Contrary to Defendant's argument, Plaintiffs are not required to plead specific facts affording an inference that Defendant's conduct resulted in Decedent's injuries. To the contrary, "[i]n the ordinary personal injury lawsuit, in which the complaint's factual recitations show plainly the connection between cause and effect, it suffices to plead causation succinctly and generally." (Bockrath v. Aldrich Chemical Co. (1990) 21 Cal.4th 78, 80; see also Jones v. ConocoPhillips Co. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1187, 1194-1197 [trial court erred in sustaining demurrers without leave to amend where allegations of causation were sufficient under Bockrath].) Additional factual details may be obtained through discovery. Accordantly, Defendant's demurrer to the First Cause of Action is overruled. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 5)

rulings.Case Title: LINDA L. FEE ET AL VS. BLOCK DRUG COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Strike And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs; Prayer For Punitive Damages
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 28, 2025 in Department 304, Line 2. 2. Defendant LVMH Perfumes and Cosmetic's Inc.'s Motion to Strike Plaintiff' Prayer for Punitive Damages is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. Defendant's request for judicial notice, Exhibit 1, is granted. (C.C.P. Sections 451, 452, 453.) As stated above, Defendant's motion is duplicative of issues already addressed and resolved by this court in its prior ruling in the matter of Christina Ingels as Successor-in-Interest to Lita Fee vs. BWDAC et al., Case No. CGC-24-277251, on January 3, 2025. The Court finds that the issues raised in this Motion are identical to those previously adjudicated. The Court emphasizes that relitigating issues already ruled upon wastes judicial resources and unnecessarily delays the resolution of this case. Defendant is cautioned against bringing further motions raising issues already decided without a proper showing under the applicable standards. To state a claim for punitive damages, plaintiff must allege specific facts showing defendant has been guilty of malice, oppression or fraud. (Smith v. Superior Court (1992) 10 Cal. App. 4th 1033, 1042.) A motion to strike may lie where the facts alleged, if proven, would not support a finding that the defendant acted with malice, fraud or oppression. (Turman v. Turning Point of Central California (2010) 191 Cal. App. 4th 53, 63.) Plaintiffs have pled sufficient facts to support a claim of punitive damages under C.C.P. section 3294(a.) Plaintiffs allege that they were exposed to asbestos from Defendant's supplied asbestos-containing talc products. Plaintiffs have alleged that Defendants (including Defendant LVMH Perfumes and Cosmetics Inc.) manufactured, supplied, and sold asbestos-containing products with knowledge of high risk of injury and death resulting from exposure to asbestos products, knew that members of the general public such as Plaintiffs would be exposed to asbestos and knew that individuals would assume that the asbestos products were safe, failed to provide a warning, and continued to market and sell products with knowledge of harm to human health. (Complaint at 29, 32-34, 36-39, 79-80.) These allegations, if proved, could support an award of punitive damages. (See SKF Farms v. Superior Court (1984) 153 Cal.App.3d 902, 906-907; see also Bankhead v. ArvinMeritor, Inc. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 68, 85 ["intentionally marketing a defective product knowing that it might cause injury and death is 'highly reprehensible.'" (cleaned up); Pfeifer v. John Crane, Inc. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1270, 1300-1302.) Defendant cites Morgan v. J-M Manufacturing Co., Inc. (2021) 60 Cal.App.5th 1078 to support its Motion. In that case, the court reversed an award of punitive damages following a jury verdict because there was no substantial evidence in the record that "J-MM's officers, directors, and managing agents acted with the requisite state of mind to support an award of punitive damages." (Id. at p. 1091.) At the demurrer stage, however, the Court's inquiry is limited to the sufficiency of the allegations in the Complaint. At this stage, the Court cannot determine as a matter of law that those allegations are insufficient to support Plaintiffs' prayer for punitive damages. Whether an officer, director or managing agent of Defendant was involved in the conduct of which Plaintiffs complain can be determined during discovery. Accordingly, Defendant's motion to strike is denied. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: LINDA L. FEE ET AL VS. BLOCK DRUG COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY - PART 4 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: (Tentative Ruling: Part 4 of 5) (Tentative Ruling continued from previous entry) Fourth Causes of Action The demurrer to the Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Nondisclosure on the ground of failure to plead with sufficient specificity or particularly is overruled. Although Plaintiffs have not alleged misrepresentations by Defendant, they can base a cause of action on fraud by nondisclosure. To establish fraud through nondisclosure or concealment of facts, it is necessary to show the defendant "was under a legal duty to disclose them." (OCM Principal Opportunities Fund v. CIBC World Markets Corp. (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 835, 845.) Nondisclosure or concealment may constitute actionable fraud when: (1) there is a fiduciary relationship between the parties; (2) the defendant had exclusive knowledge of material facts not known to the plaintiff; (3) the defendant actively conceals a material fact from the plaintiff; and (4) the defendant makes partial representations but also suppresses some material facts. (Bigler-Engler v. Breg, Inc. (2017) 7 Cal.App.5th 276, 310-311.) However, a fiduciary relationship need not exist between the parties when the parties are in a relationship that gives rise to the duty, such as seller and buyer. (Id. at 311.) Such a relationship may arise from a transaction between the parties that involves direct dealings between the plaintiff and the defendant. (Id. at 311-312, citing authorities.) Defendant argues that Plaintiffs have not alleged a single fact establishing a "fiduciary" or "confidential" relationship between Plaintiffs and Defendant. However, as discussed above, such a relationship is not required. Allegations that the defendant was involved in retail sales of the disputed product to consumers are sufficient to establish a sufficient transaction or relationship between the parties. (Bader v. Johnson & Johnson (2022) 86 Cal.App.5th 1094, 1130-1132; see also Jones, 198 Cal.App.4th at 1198-1200 [complaint adequately alleged cause of action for fraudulent concealment where plaintiffs alleged that defendant manufacturers were aware of the toxic nature of their products and owed a duty to disclose those properties].) Here, the Complaint alleges that Defendant marketed, manufactured, sold, and supplied asbestos-containing products. (Complaint at 21.) Plaintiffs further allege that Defendant sold its products directly to Plaintiffs, thereby creating a direct transactional relationship between Defendant and Plaintiffs giving rise to a duty to disclose. (Id. at 66). "Although sparse, nothing more is required at this stage of the litigation." (Jones, 198 Cal.App.4th at 1200.) Accordingly, Defendant's demurrer to the Fourth Cause of Action is overruled. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 5 of 5)

rulings.Case Title: LINDA L. FEE ET AL VS. BLOCK DRUG COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY - PART 2 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: (Tentative Ruling: Part 2 of 5) (Tentative Ruling continued from previous entry) Second Cause of Action The demurrer to the Second Cause of Action for Strict Liability on the ground of failure to state a cause of action is overruled. Defendant argues Plaintiffs' Complaint fails to identify any specific product used by the Decedent. The Court disagrees. Plaintiffs are not required to allege the specific product or products for which Defendant is responsible. The ruling in Bockrath v. Aldrich Chem. Co. (1990) 21 Cal. 4th 71, 80, applies only when the pleaded facts of negligence and injury do not naturally give rise to an inference of causation, such as in a case involving numerous defendants who are sued for products liability based on long-term exposure to multiple toxins. (See Bockrath, 21 Cal.4th at 77 [Court granted review "to decide how a complaint alleging harmful long-term exposure to multiple toxins must plead causation."].) In this case, Plaintiffs allege that they were exposed to a single toxin-asbestos-and that Defendant's products contained asbestos to which Decedent was exposed. (Complaint at 1, 5-8, 11, 15-17.) Moreover, the Complaint alleges with specificity that Ms. Fee was a lifelong user of cosmetic and personal hygiene talcum powder products. She applies these powder products to her face and body on a regular basis. Mr. Fee also routinely used talcum powder products in the family home shared with Ms. Fee. (See Exhibit A to Plaintiffs' Complaint.) The nature of Plaintiffs' claim against Defendant is clear. As discussed above, there is no requirement that claims for negligence and strict products liability be alleged with particularity, and Plaintiffs are not required to include evidentiary details in the Complaint. Accordingly, Defendant's demurrer to the Second Cause of Action is overruled. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 3 of 5)

rulings.Case Title: LINDA L. FEE ET AL VS. BLOCK DRUG COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT LVMH PERFUMES AND COSMETICS INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY - PART 3 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: (Tentative Ruling: Part 3 of 5) (Tentative Ruling continued from previous entry) Third Causes of Action The demurrer to the Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation on the ground of failure to plead with sufficient specificity or particularly is sustained without leave to amend. The Court agrees with Defendant that Plaintiffs fail to specifically plead facts that meet the heightened pleading requirements for the Third Cause of Action. In addition, the Court notes Plaintiffs' Opposition does not address the Negligent Misrepresentation Cause of Action. In an action for negligent misrepresentation, a plaintiff is required to show the same elements as intentional fraud, i.e., "(1) false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure; (2) knowledge of falsity; (3) intent to defraud; (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) damage," except they need not show elements two or three, i.e., "scienter or intent to defraud." (Engalla v. Permanente Medical Group, Inc. (1997) 15 Cal.4th 951, 974; Small v. Fritz Companies, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 167, 174.) The facts that constitute the fraud must be alleged fully, factually, and specifically. (Wilhelm v. Pray, Price, Williams & Russell (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1324, 1331.) The particularity requirement necessitates the pleading facts that show how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representation was tendered, and pleading specifically the detriment proximately caused by the defendant's conduct. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 73; Service of Medallion, Inc. v. Clorox Co. (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 1807, 1818.) When alleging fraud against a corporation, the plaintiff must allege the names of the persons who made the allegedly fraudulent representation, their authority to speak, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when it was said or written, unless by the allegations the defendant necessarily possesses full information on the facts or when the facts lie more in the knowledge of the defendant. (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 645; Tarmann v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. (1991) 2 Cal.App.4th 153, 157.) Here, Plaintiffs fail to plead fraud with specificity against Defendant. The Complaint fails to set forth any facts as to alleged misrepresentations made by Defendant to Plaintiffs, the names of the persons who made the allegedly fraudulent representations, their authority to speak, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when it was said or written. Instead, Plaintiffs attribute the purported misrepresentations to all co-defendants as a group, which is insufficient to meet the heightened pleading standards for fraud against a corporate defendant. Moreover, the Third Cause of Action is fatally defective. Plaintiffs' Opposition does not provide the Court with any hints as to how Plaintiffs would propose to cure the fatal defects. Accordingly, the demurrer to Third Cause of Action is sustained without leave to amend. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 4 of 5)
